name: Zubat
route: Mt. Moon, Rock Tunnel, Seafoam caves, dark mines, bridge undersides, and abandoned buildings
habitat: marshes, scrub, waste sites, damp caves, rotting logs, and contaminated urban edges; also uses open fields, cliffs, route corridors, tall trees, rooftops, and thermal ridges. Field anchors: Mt. Moon, Rock Tunnel, Seafoam caves, dark mines, bridge undersides, and abandoned buildings
behaviorTemperament: Zubat is best treated as a base field organism with poison-type instincts. In the wild it prioritizes food, shelter, breeding security, and escape routes before accepting contact. Stress signs usually appear before attack, but handlers often miss them because the Pokemon's powers look theatrical rather than biological.
diet: small prey, carrion, insects, contaminated plant matter, and toxin-rich foods handled through specialized organs; secondary needs include seeds, insects, fish, rodents, eggs, fruit, and carrion depending on beak shape
activityPattern: Diurnal, with dawn and late-afternoon patrols. Roosting activity increases near sunset.
bestTimeToFind: Morning thermals, high perches, route signs, cliffs, rooftops, or fields with prey movement
observationDifficulty: Moderate. Common field signs are visible, but direct observation can still trigger flight or defense.
populationStatus: Stable where habitat remains intact, but local pressure increases near roads, farms, mines, labs, and tourist routes.
description: A Poison / Flying Pokemon listed as Bat Pokemon. This real-world field profile keeps its game powers intact while treating them as biological hazards shaped by habitat, stress, and survival needs.
lore: Before Zubat was treated as a cataloged species, travelers in its range reported small but repeatable hazards: disturbed camps, strange tracks, injured livestock, missing food, and warning signs that matched no ordinary animal. Later surveys tied those reports to Mt. Moon, Rock Tunnel, Seafoam caves, dark mines, bridge undersides, and abandoned buildings. The species is now handled as a Poison / Flying field organism whose powers are real but still shaped by habitat, food, breeding pressure, and fear. Old local accounts often exaggerate Zubat into a monster. The safer interpretation is more practical: it is an animal with extraordinary biology, and most injuries happen when humans treat a warning display as curiosity instead of a boundary.
survivalUse: Can help a trained ranger locate habitat clues tied to poison activity, such as food sources, shelter, water, weather changes, or dangerous terrain. Usefulness ends quickly if the animal is frightened, hungry, nesting, injured, or evolving.
handlingWarning: Do not corner Zubat, handle it casually, or force contact during feeding, nesting, evolution stress, or injury. Main hazards are venom, irritant fluid, and contaminated surfaces. Maintain retreat space and use trained handlers for capture or care.
encounterAdvice: Stay upwind of Zubat, keep skin covered, avoid touching shed material, and withdraw if it displays venom, hooding, barbs, colored fluid, or warning posture.
firstAid: For suspected venom or toxin contact, remove contaminated clothing, rinse with clean water, keep the victim calm, mark swelling spread, and seek urgent medical care.
companionSuitability: Moderate suitability with careful training, habitat control, and emergency planning
fieldBehaviors: Displays warning colors before venom use
Avoids direct sun when toxins dry out
fieldNote: A calm Zubat usually gives space before contact. A sudden change in sound, posture, glow, scent, temperature, or eye focus should be treated as the final warning before a poison-type response.
originStory: The Zubat Evolution Family appears to have adapted around Mt. Moon, Rock Tunnel, Seafoam caves, dark mines, bridge undersides, and abandoned buildings. Its line favors survival through venom, irritant fluid, and contaminated surfaces, specialized shelter choice, and rapid behavioral changes during maturity. Zubat becomes Golbat by Level 22. In field terms, each stage is best read as a different ecological job: the youngest form survives by hiding, warning, or fleeing; intermediate forms defend territory and gather resources; final forms dominate the local niche or stabilize the colony. This dossier treats evolution as a real biological threshold triggered by growth, stress, energy reserves, and the listed game-derived method.

evolutionLine.name: Zubat
evolutionLine.note: Base-stage Poison / Flying species. Growth, training, survival stress, and the listed method lead toward the next form.

evolutionLine.name: Golbat
evolutionLine.note: Final-stage Poison / Flying species within the first-151 field scope. Manage as a mature adult, not a juvenile.

mainLine.name: Zubat
mainLine.note: Base-stage Poison / Flying species. Growth, training, survival stress, and the listed method lead toward the next form.

mainLine.name: Golbat
mainLine.note: Final-stage Poison / Flying species within the first-151 field scope. Manage as a mature adult, not a juvenile.

domesticationStatus: Mostly wild with licensed handling possible
prey: small prey, carrion, insects, contaminated plant matter, and toxin-rich foods handled through specialized organs; secondary needs include seeds, insects, fish, rodents, eggs, fruit, and carrion depending on beak shape
temperamentTriggers: Improper response to poison-type warning signs
territoryRange: Less than 1 square mile around shelter and feeding sites
lifeCycle: Hatches or emerges as a juvenile adapted to marshes, feeds heavily, defends shelter, and follows this first-151 evolution profile: Zubat evolves into Golbat by Level 22.
containmentRequirements: Requires negative-pressure space, washable flooring, and antidote planning, food control, shelter enrichment, double-door access, species-specific medical plan, and emergency standoff based on its listed attacks.
ecologicalRole: Fills a poison-type niche by regulating prey, plants, waste, terrain, weather pressure, or predator behavior in its native habitat. Removing it can cause local imbalance even when the species is hazardous.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Concerns include wound infection, parasites from native habitat, stress illness, and secondary exposure related to envenomation, swelling, numbness, contaminated clothing, and delayed systemic symptoms
reproductionNesting: Nests or shelters in protected parts of its habitat. Eggs or young are usually guarded more strongly during storms, drought, cold snaps, food shortage, or evolution season.
predators: Larger birds, snakes, aquatic predators, stronger territorial Pokemon, humans, disease, and habitat loss
emergencyResponse: Secure bystanders, stop pursuit, move to cover, and treat the scene as a poison hazard area. Do not attempt capture unless trained and equipped for Zubat.
legalStatus: Permit recommended or required in settled areas, especially for transport, breeding, public display, or combat training
lifespan: Official lifespan is not stated. Field estimate: wild 2 to 6 years; managed care 10 to 20 years depending on species size and evolution stage.
socialStructure: pairs, loose colonies, or defended territories

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Main hazard comes from body contact and species-specific attacks.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: No electric field. Maintain normal standoff based on speed, terrain, and behavior.
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Field burst speed roughly 13 to 24 mph depending on terrain and age
fieldCapabilities.jumpingHeight: Variable by body plan; assume sudden lunges over short obstacles are possible
fieldCapabilities.balance: Strong in native habitat; reduced on slick artificial flooring or unfamiliar confinement

trainingRisk: Training risk includes envenomation, swelling, numbness, contaminated clothing, and delayed systemic symptoms. Evolution-stage changes can make a formerly safe individual unsafe without warning.
physiology: Zubat's body supports Poison / Flying output through specialized organs, defensive tissues, and stress-responsive behavior. Its attacks are not magic tricks in this guide; they are treated as biological systems that consume energy, create waste heat or toxins, and fail when the animal is sick.

keyFacts.diet: small prey, carrion, insects, contaminated plant matter, and toxin-rich foods handled through specialized organs; secondary needs include seeds, insects, fish, rodents, eggs, fruit, and carrion depending on beak shape
keyFacts.habitat: marshes, scrub, waste sites, damp caves, rotting logs, and contaminated urban edges
keyFacts.lifespan: Official lifespan not stated; see lifespan field for field estimate.
keyFacts.groupSize: small groups, pairs, or local colonies depending on habitat

physicalCharacteristics: Recognizable as bat pokemon with poison / flying adaptations, field coloration, specialized attack structures, and body proportions matching its official Pokédex profile.
dietRestrictions: Avoid human junk food, contaminated prey, unfamiliar supplements, forced fasting, and habitat-inappropriate foods. Diet changes should be gradual and matched to type biology.

attacks.name: Poison Sting
attacks.fieldUse: venom prick or spine jab used to test distance and discourage handling

attacks.name: Acid
attacks.fieldUse: caustic spray used against plants, cloth, and soft tissue

attacks.name: Toxic
attacks.fieldUse: persistent venom contamination used to make predators back away

attacks.name: Sludge Bomb
attacks.fieldUse: thick toxic projectile that contaminates soil and gear

Kanto: Mt. Moon, Rock Tunnel, Seafoam caves, dark mines, bridge undersides, and abandoned buildings
JohtoAndLater: Johto, Hoenn, Sinnoh, Unova, Kalos, Alola, and later-region appearances vary by game version and habitat compatibility
realWorldAnalog: Use matching terrain analogs rather than real geography alone: old forests, caves, coasts, wetlands, mountains, power corridors, urban margins, or protected preserves depending on the species.

officialEvolutionSummary.summary: Zubat evolves into Golbat by Level 22.

sourceUseNote: Core names, numbering, typing, height, weight, base stats, and first-151 evolution methods are based on public Pokedex and game database references. Lifespan, ecology, first aid, containment, and real-world behavior are interpretive survival-guide material for this passion project.
